title: 🎉 Add Mythpen
author: niyongsheng
body: ### 🪩 Provide a link to the proposed addition https://github.com/niyongsheng/mythpen https://niyongsheng.github.io/mythpen/index.html ### 😳 Explain why it should be added Mythpen is an open-source AI-assisted novel writing desktop app built with **React 19 + Tauri v2 + Express 5**. Unlike generic AI chat clients, its AI agent can directly read and write project data — create chapters, manage character profiles, build world settings, arrange foreshadowing, and maintain timelines — all persisted to a local SQLite database via function calling. **Key features:** - **Writing Mode** (6-step workflow): AI scans chapter statuses, reads context, writes the next chapter, and auto-advances — like a tireless writing partner. - **Collab Mode** (4-step workflow): Brainstorm ideas → world-building → volume planning → deliver first chapters. - **Narrative Dimensions**: Each chapter tracks 5 dimensions (cognitive frame, emotional anchor, world texture, concrete mystery, interpersonal tension). - **Dual AI Provider**: Supports both OpenAI-compatible APIs (works with domestic Chinese models) and Claude API, auto-detected. - **Cross-platform**: macOS / Windows / Linux (Tauri v2, not Electron) - **i18n**: Full English and Chinese localization ### 📖 Additional context **Tech stack**: React 19 + TypeScript 6 + Tailwind CSS 4 + Zustand / Express 5 + SQLite (sql.js) / Tauri v2 **License**: GPLv3 ### 🧨 Issue Checklist - [x] I have checked for other similar issues - [x] I have explained why this change is important - [x] I have added necessary documentation (if appropriate)

title: 🎉 Add MrEditor
author: MR-TABATA
body:
### 🪩 Provide a link to the proposed addition

https://mr-tabata.github.io/MrEditor/
https://github.com/MR-TABATA/MrEditor

### 😳 Explain why it should be added

> **Correction (2026-07-12).** I originally filed this issue with a memory figure of "44 MB",
> and with a first-paint time of "~210 ms" and an index time of "~20 s". Those came from a
> benchmark run on a pre-1.0 build and I reused them without re-measuring. I have re-measured
> everything on the shipping 1.0.2 release build and corrected the numbers below. The speed
> figures were wrong in my favour by ~2–3x (it is faster), and the memory figure was wrong
> against me: the app's footprint is **162 MB**, not 44 MB. I would rather fix it here than
> have you find it.

MrEditor is a Mac-native (AppKit, no Electron) text editor built for the files other editors refuse: it opens a **10 GB log — 86,420,337 lines — in 65–83 ms**, holds **0 bytes of that file** in memory (it is mmap'd, never copied — `vmmap` reports 0 dirty bytes for the mapping), and **edits and saves it in place** with atomic writes.

It is MIT-licensed, free, universal (Apple Silicon + Intel), signed with an Apple Developer ID and notarized, so it opens with a plain double-click. macOS 13+.

Why it earns a line in the list:

- **It fills a real gap in Text Editors.** `NSTextView` keeps the whole document in `NSTextStorage`, so every editor built on it falls over at gigabyte scale. Nothing currently in the section handles multi-gigabyte files; the tools that come close (klogg, lnav) are read-only log viewers and are not Mac-native. MrEditor mmaps the file, keeps a sparse line index, draws only the visible lines with Core Text, and represents edits as a piece table — so inserting a character at line 1 of a 10 GB file does not rewrite 10 GB.
- **It edits, not just views.** Full-file search, regex, filtered view (live grep) and `tail -f` follow, plus a structured view (CSV/TSV column alignment, NDJSON field projection).
- **It is genuinely CJK-first.** IME input in the editor, encoding detection, Shift_JIS / EUC-JP conversion on save, CRLF/CR/LF handling — unusual for this category, and relevant to a good part of this list's readership.

**Honest limitations**, since I'd rather you hear them from me: the 80 ms is *time to first paint*. The full line index of that 10 GB file takes about **9 seconds**; it builds in the background and never blocks the view, but the exact line count and full-file search only settle once it finishes. While indexing, `ps` RSS climbs to several GB — that is reclaimable, file-backed mmap page cache, not resident app memory (`vmmap` reports 0 dirty bytes for it) — but it does look alarming in Activity Monitor. The app's own 162 MB footprint is window backing store and the kernel page tables for a 10 GB mapping, not your log. The app is also young: 1.0 shipped 2026-07-11.

I am the author.

### 📖 Additional context

Proposed entry for **Reading and Writing Tools > Text Editors**, in the existing style of the section (alphabetically between `micro` and `Neovim`):

```markdown
* [MrEditor](https://mr-tabata.github.io/MrEditor/) - "A Mac-native viewer — and editor — for 10 GB text files." [![Open-Source Software][OSS Icon]](https://github.com/MR-TABATA/MrEditor) ![Freeware][Freeware Icon]
```

A 10 GB, 86,420,337-line log open in MrEditor:

![A 10 GB, 86,420,337-line log open in MrEditor](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/MR-TABATA/MrEditor/main/docs/img/10gb-dark.png)

Measured on a 10.00 GB / 86,420,337-line Japanese UTF-8 log, on the shipping universal release build (2026-07-12):

| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Time to first paint | 65–83 ms |
| The file's own pages | 4.2 GB resident, **0 bytes dirty** |
| App physical footprint | 162 MB with the file open (32 MB with nothing open) |
| Seek to last line | 0.1 ms |
| Full background index | ~9 s (does not block display) |
| Full-file search | 4.59 M hits |

Read the middle two rows together: the 10 GB costs nothing (mapped, not copied, 0 dirty bytes), and the 162 MB the app does use is window buffers and page tables — not the file. Reproduce it with:

```sh
MREDITOR_TIMING=1 MrEditor.app/Contents/MacOS/MrEditor big.log   # → first paint: 81.3 ms
vmmap $(pgrep -x MrEditor) | grep big.log                        # → 10.0G  4.2G  0K
```

Benchmark table and more screenshots: https://github.com/MR-TABATA/MrEditor#performance

Happy to open the PR myself and update `README.md`, `README-zh.md`, `README-ja.md` and `README-ko.md` together if you'd prefer that — just say the word. Japanese description, if useful: `"10GB のテキストファイルを開いて編集できる、Mac ネイティブのエディタ。"`

title: Add DiskSage to Cleanup and Uninstall
author: stealthdev-labs
body:
**DiskSage** — open-source macOS disk cleaner that explains *why* each file is safe to delete.

- Sunburst map, duplicate finder, biggest-files view
- Safety verdicts on every folder (Safe / Review / Keep)
- 100% offline, no telemetry, MIT license
- Trash-only (never hard deletes)

https://github.com/stealthdev-labs/disksage

title: Feature Request: Integrate Crowdin for community translations
author: Bwillou1
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Yes. The project is growing and already has a few manual localized versions (Chinese, Korean, Japanese). However, managing translations manually through git forks and copies of markdown files creates a lot of friction and potential merge conflicts for the community.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would love to see Crowdin integrated into the repository. 

Since the project relies heavily on Markdown files, Crowdin can easily split the text into segments. This would allow the community (including the Francophone community and many others) to contribute translations directly through a clean web interface without needing to manually copy files or handle git conflicts. Crowdin can then automatically open Pull Requests to sync the updated localized files back to GitHub. 

https://store.crowdin.com/github
https://crowdin.com/

### Describe alternatives you've considered

The alternative is keeping the current workflow (creating manual files like `README-fr.md` via standard Pull Requests), but it scales poorly as more languages are added and the main English README gets updated.

### 📖 Additional contex

I would be more than happy to help kickstart and maintain the French translation on Crowdin if you decide to set it up!

### 🧨 Issue Checklist

- [x] I have checked for other similar issues
- [x] I have explained why this change is important
- [x] I have added necessary documentation (if appropriate)

title: 🎉 Add NeZha
author: hanshuaikang
body: ### 🪩 Provide a link to the proposed addition https://github.com/hanshuaikang/nezha ### 😳 Explain why it should be added A lightweight IDE built for the AI agents era — run multiple Claude Code and Codex agents across projects, right on your machine. - Run Claude Code & Codex side by side - Multi-project workspace with one-click switching - Cross-project task timeline at a glance - Real-time terminal output, no log digging - Auto-discover and replay agent sessions (JSONL) - Permission-aware execution: Ask · Auto-edit · Full access - Built-in Git: branches, diff, commit, AI commit message - Embedded shell terminal & file explorer - Token & tool-call analytics per session - Light, fast, native — Tauri + Rust, not Electron ### 📖 Additional context ### 🧨 Issue Checklist - [x] I have checked for other similar issues - [x] I have explained why this change is important - [x] I have added necessary documentation (if appropriate)

title: Add claudio: system-wide macOS voice dictation via Claude subscription
author: ayushkumar1808
body:
**Name:** claudio

**Description:** System-wide voice dictation for macOS. Hold Space in any app to start recording; release to commit the transcript. Uses Deepgram Nova 3 via Anthropic's WebSocket API. No new subscriptions or API keys needed if you already use Claude Code.

**Link:** https://github.com/ayushkumar1808/claudio

**Category:** Developer Tools / Input Methods / Productivity

**License:** MIT

title: 🎉 Add Mystral
author: fexxdev
body: ### 🪩 Provide a link to the proposed addition https://github.com/fexxdev/Mystral ### 😳 Explain why it should be added Mystral is a free, open-source (MIT) native macOS menu bar app that gives Apple Silicon Mac users full control over their fan curves, something macOS doesn't offer natively. It supports every Apple Silicon generation from M1 through M5, including a novel SMC unlock technique for M3/M4+ chips where Apple firmware-locks manual fan control. Key highlights: - Drag-and-drop fan curve editor with live temperature/RPM charts - Smart profile auto-switching based on power source (AC/Battery) or thermal state - 4 built-in presets (Silent, Balanced, Performance, Full Blast) plus custom profiles - Production-grade reliability: EMA smoothing, hysteresis, helper health monitoring, wake-from-sleep handling - Fully offline & private — zero telemetry, no network access, all data stored locally - Built with Swift 6 / SwiftUI, clean architecture, active CI/CD, and a comprehensive test suite There is currently no comparable open-source fan control tool for Apple Silicon in the awesome-mac list. Mystral fills that gap with a polished, well-engineered solution that rivals commercial alternatives. ### 📖 Additional context ### 🧨 Issue Checklist - [x] I have checked for other similar issues - [x] I have explained why this change is important - [x] I have added necessary documentation (if appropriate)

title: 🎉 Add Xray
author: wlswo
body:
### 🪩 Provide a link to the proposed addition

https://github.com/wlswo/xray

### 😳 Explain why it should be added

Xray is a macOS-only UI inspection tool — think browser DevTools, but for any element on your screen. Hover over any
UI element to see its role and dimensions in real time, and capture it to the clipboard with a single click. It runs
in the background via the system tray and is activated with a global shortcut (Cmd+Shift+X).

Built with Tauri (Rust + React/TypeScript), MIT-licensed, and actively maintained. It's a handy utility for Mac
designers and developers who frequently need to inspect or document native UI, and I think it would be a good fit for
 the Applications → Developer (or Utilities) section of awesome-mac.

### 📖 Additional context

- Latest release: v1.1.0 (Jan 2026)
- Multi-language support: English, Korean, Chinese, Japanese
- Requires Accessibility and Screen Recording permissions
- 264+ stars on GitHub

title: 🎉 Add Wox
author: qianlifeng
body:
### 🪩 Provide a link to the proposed addition

https://github.com/Wox-launcher/Wox

### 😳 Explain why it should be added

Wox is an open-source cross-platform launcher for macOS, Linux, and Windows. It provides fast local search for apps, files, folders, and commands, supports extensibility through Python and JavaScript plugins, includes theme support, and can be installed on macOS with Homebrew.

It is a practical alternative to Alfred, Raycast, and Spotlight-style launchers, and would fit well in Awesome Mac's Productivity category.

![https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Wox-launcher/Wox/refs/heads/master/screenshots/glance.png](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Wox-launcher/Wox/refs/heads/master/screenshots/glance.png)

### 📖 Additional context

Official documentation: https://wox-launcher.github.io/Wox/
macOS install: `brew install --cask wox`
License: GPL-3.0

title: Add PaperClaw — Scientific Paper Writing for VS Code
author: Agnuxo1
body:
## Proposal: Add PaperClaw — VS Code Extension for Scientific Paper Writing

**Extension:** https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=agnuxo1.paperclaw
**Repository:** https://github.com/Agnuxo1/paperclaw-extension
**License:** Apache 2.0

### What it does
PaperClaw is a **VS Code extension that transforms the editor into a scientific paper writing environment** with AI-powered assistance:

- **Paper Mode** — Dedicated workspace for academic writing with structured sections
- **AI-Powered Writing** — Integrated with CAJAL model for local paper generation
- **Citation Management** — BibTeX / CSL-JSON support with automatic formatting
- **Tribunal Review** — Built-in AI peer review scoring (5 dimensions)
- **LaTeX Export** — One-click compilation to PDF via pandoc/latex
- **IPFS Publish** — Direct publish to P2PCLAW decentralized network
- **Offline-First** — All processing local, no cloud dependency

### Why it fits this list
- VS Code extension specifically designed for **academic/scientific writing**
- Local-first AI writing assistant (privacy-preserving)
- Part of broader P2PCLAW ecosystem (paper: arXiv:2604.19792)
- Integrates with CAJAL local LLM for paper generation

### Links
- https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=agnuxo1.paperclaw
- https://github.com/Agnuxo1/paperclaw-extension
- https://github.com/Agnuxo1/CAJAL

---
*Happy to open a PR if preferred. Thanks for this excellent resource!*

title: 🎉 Add Plexus
author: miniLV
body:
### 🪩 Provide a link to the proposed addition
https://github.com/miniLV/Plexus

## App Name

Plexus

## Category

AI Tools / Developer Utilities

## Description

Plexus is a local-first dashboard for managing AI coding agent configuration on macOS.

It helps developers sync Rules, MCP servers, and Skills across tools like Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Gemini CLI, Qwen Code, and Factory Droid. Instead of manually copying the same `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, MCP config, and skill folders between different AI coding tools, Plexus keeps one canonical local store under `~/.config/plexus/` and projects it back into each agent's native location.

## Why it is useful for macOS users

Many macOS developers now use more than one AI coding tool. Each tool has its own config files and folder structure, so configuration easily drifts over time.

Plexus provides:

- one local dashboard for all detected AI agents
- one-click import from existing agent configs
- one-click sync to enabled agents
- support for Rules, MCP servers, and Skills
- backup snapshots before writing native files
- symlink-based sharing where safe
- partial writes for shared native config files, preserving auth/history/profile data
- optional team config repo support

## Screenshots

![Plexus Dashboard](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/miniLV/Plexus/master/docs/assets/readme/dashboard.png)

![Plexus Architecture](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/miniLV/Plexus/master/docs/assets/readme/config-sharing-map.png)

## License

Apache-2.0

## Platform

macOS / Linux

## Notes

Plexus is currently distributed as a Node.js/Next.js local dashboard and CLI, not a native `.app` bundle yet.


### 😳 Explain why it should be added

Plexus should be added because many macOS developers use multiple AI coding tools, but each tool has separate rules, MCP config, and skills folders.

Plexus gives them one local dashboard to import, sync, and back up those configs across Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, Gemini CLI, and other agents. It is a practical developer utility for managing AI coding workflows on macOS.


### 📖 Additional context

_No response_
